title: The price of a copper pot
summary: In a Marrakech souk, a young vendor who wants to put fixed price tags on the family stall argues with the older uncle who has bargained for forty years — about what is really being sold when a price is haggled.

body.speaker: Younes
body.text: Uncle Brahim, I have done the numbers. If we put a fixed price on every pot and lamp, we sell faster, the tourists trust us more, and nobody walks away feeling cheated. The shop two doors down did it, and their sales rose.

body.speaker: Uncle Brahim
body.text: Their sales have gone up this season. Sit. Before you change a way of selling older than your father, tell me: when a tourist haggles for that copper pot, what has he bought?

body.speaker: Uncle Brahim
body.text: No. He could buy one at home, by machine, for the same money. What he has bought is fifteen minutes with me — the story he will tell: how the old man started high, how he walked away twice, how we shook hands. The pot is the proof. The haggle is the thing.

body.speaker: Younes
body.text: I am not saying the story is worthless. But you are picturing the tourist who enjoys the game. Half of them hate it. They do not know the rules, they are afraid of being cheated, they cannot tell if your 'final price' is fair, so they overpay and feel foolish, or panic and leave with nothing. We lose those sales.

body.speaker: Uncle Brahim
body.text: That is true. You have named the real cost, and I will not pretend it away. The frightened customer is one we lose, and there are more of them now. A tag would catch them.

body.speaker: Uncle Brahim
body.text: We agree on the problem, not yet on the cure. Think what the tag also removes. When I haggle, I am reading the person. The newlyweds on honeymoon, I let win, and they tell forty friends. The bored rich man, I make work for it, because the work is what he came for. The student counting coins, I find a smaller pot and let him keep his dignity. A printed number says the same to all four — fair, but fairness, here, is a kind of blindness.

body.speaker: Younes
body.text: Then we are each half right, and that is uncomfortable. The tag is fair and welcoming and loses no frightened customers — but it cannot read a face. The haggle reads the face and sells a story — but loses the people most afraid of being cheated.

body.speaker: Uncle Brahim
body.text: Yes. So perhaps we do not choose. The small things — cheap lamps, keyrings, the tea glasses tourists buy by the dozen — get a tag, because nobody wants a story about a keyring. And the real pots keep the old way. A tag for the things that are only things; the haggle for the things that are also a meeting.

body.speaker: Younes
body.text: That I can do the numbers for. But you must teach me to read a face, Uncle, because I have only ever learned to read a spreadsheet.

body.speaker: Uncle Brahim
body.text: That, my boy, takes longer than a season, and cannot be done with a calculator. Pour the tea. We will start with the next tourist who touches the copper, and I will tell you, before he opens his mouth, whether he wants the pot or the story.

annotations.meaningVi: chính việc mặc cả mới là điều cốt lõi
vờ như nó không tồn tại
giữ thể diện / lòng tự trọng
một kiểu mù lòa (không nhìn ra khác biệt)
một cuộc gặp gỡ (giữa người với người)

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for results + second conditional for hypotheses
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để nói về kết quả còn ảnh hưởng tới hiện tại: 'their sales have gone up', 'I have done the numbers again', 'what has he bought?'. Bài cũng dùng câu điều kiện loại 1 cho hệ quả có thật ('If we put a fixed price on every pot... we sell faster') và cách diễn đạt giả định/đề xuất với 'perhaps we do not choose' và 'A tag would catch them' (would = sẽ, trong giả định). Chú ý mệnh đề quan hệ rút gọn: 'the things that are only things' / 'the things that are also a meeting'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses the present perfect for results that still matter now: 'their sales have gone up', 'I have done the numbers again', 'what has he bought?'. It also uses the first conditional for a real consequence ('If we put a fixed price on every pot... we sell faster') and hypothetical 'would': 'A tag would catch them'. Note the relative clauses contrasting 'the things that are only things' with 'the things that are also a meeting'.

translationVi:
Younes: Chú Brahim, cháu đã tính sổ. Nếu mình niêm yết giá cố định cho mọi cái nồi và đèn, mình bán nhanh hơn, khách du lịch tin mình hơn, và không ai bỏ đi với cảm giác bị lừa. Cửa hàng cách hai nhà đã làm vậy, và doanh số họ tăng.

Uncle Brahim: Doanh số họ tăng mùa này. Ngồi đi. Trước khi cháu thay đổi một cách bán còn già hơn cả cha cháu, nói cho chú: khi một du khách mặc cả cái nồi đồng kia, anh ta đã mua gì?

Younes: Một cái nồi đồng.

Uncle Brahim: Không. Anh ta có thể mua một cái ở nhà, bằng máy, với cùng số tiền. Cái anh ta mua là mười lăm phút với chú — câu chuyện anh ta sẽ kể: ông già hét giá cao ra sao, anh ta bỏ đi hai lần thế nào, chúng tôi bắt tay ra sao. Cái nồi là bằng chứng. Cuộc mặc cả mới là điều cốt lõi.

Younes: Cháu không nói câu chuyện đó vô giá trị. Nhưng chú đang hình dung người du khách thích trò chơi này. Một nửa trong số họ ghét nó. Họ không biết luật, họ sợ bị lừa, họ không phân biệt được 'giá chót' của chú có công bằng không, nên họ trả quá nhiều và thấy mình ngốc, hoặc cuống lên và bỏ đi tay không. Mình mất những vụ bán đó.

Uncle Brahim: Đúng vậy. Cháu đã gọi tên cái giá thật, và chú sẽ không vờ như nó không có. Người khách sợ hãi là khách mình mất, và bây giờ có nhiều người như vậy hơn. Một cái nhãn giá sẽ giữ được họ.

Younes: Vậy là mình đồng ý.

Uncle Brahim: Mình đồng ý về vấn đề, chưa đồng ý về cách chữa. Hãy nghĩ xem cái nhãn còn lấy đi điều gì. Khi chú mặc cả, chú đang đọc con người. Đôi vợ chồng mới cưới đi trăng mật, chú để họ thắng, và họ kể cho bốn mươi người bạn. Người giàu đang chán, chú bắt phải vất vả mới mua được, vì cái sự vất vả ấy mới là thứ ông ta đến tìm. Cậu sinh viên đang đếm từng đồng, chú tìm một cái nồi nhỏ hơn và để cậu giữ thể diện. Một con số in sẵn nói cùng một điều với cả bốn — công bằng, nhưng công bằng, ở đây, là một kiểu mù lòa.

Younes: Vậy là mỗi người đúng một nửa, và điều đó thật khó chịu. Cái nhãn thì công bằng, niềm nở, và không mất khách sợ hãi nào — nhưng nó không đọc được khuôn mặt. Cuộc mặc cả thì đọc được khuôn mặt và bán được câu chuyện — nhưng nó mất những người sợ bị lừa nhất.

Uncle Brahim: Đúng. Vậy có lẽ mình không chọn. Những thứ nhỏ — đèn rẻ, móc khóa, ly trà mà khách mua cả tá — gắn nhãn, vì không ai muốn một câu chuyện về cái móc khóa. Còn những cái nồi thật giữ cách cũ. Nhãn cho những thứ chỉ là đồ vật; mặc cả cho những thứ còn là một cuộc gặp gỡ.

Younes: Cái đó cháu tính sổ được. Nhưng chú phải dạy cháu đọc khuôn mặt, chú ạ, vì cháu mới chỉ học đọc bảng tính.

Uncle Brahim: Cái đó, cháu ơi, lâu hơn một mùa, và không làm được bằng máy tính. Rót trà đi. Mình sẽ bắt đầu với du khách tiếp theo chạm vào đồ đồng, và chú sẽ nói cho cháu, trước khi anh ta mở miệng, rằng anh ta muốn cái nồi hay muốn câu chuyện.

questions.prompt: What change does Younes want to make?
options: Put a fixed price on every pot and lamp so they sell faster and tourists trust them
questions.explanation: 'If we put a fixed price on every pot and lamp, we sell faster, the tourists trust us more.'

questions.prompt: According to Uncle Brahim, what does a tourist who haggles really buy?
options: Fifteen minutes with him and the story he will tell at home; the pot is just the proof
questions.explanation: 'What he has bought is fifteen minutes with me... The pot is the proof. The haggle is the thing.'

questions.prompt: What is Younes's argument about the tourists who hate haggling?
options: They do not know the rules, fear being cheated, so they overpay and feel foolish or leave with nothing — lost sales
They prefer the shop two doors down's pots
questions.explanation: 'they are afraid of being cheated... so they pay too much and feel foolish, or they panic and leave with nothing. We lose those sales completely.'

questions.prompt: How does Uncle Brahim respond to that argument?
options: He admits it is true and names it the real cost — the frightened customer is lost, and there are more of them than before
He says those tourists do not matter
questions.explanation: 'You have named the real cost, and I will not pretend it away. The frightened customer is a customer we lose.'

questions.prompt: When Younes says 'So we agree', what does Brahim reply?
options: They agree on the problem but not yet on the cure
questions.explanation: 'We agree on the problem. Not yet on the cure.'

questions.prompt: What does Brahim say haggling lets him do that a tag cannot?
options: Read the person and treat the newlyweds, the bored rich man and the poor student differently
questions.explanation: 'When I haggle, I am reading the person,' followed by the four different customers.

questions.prompt: What does Brahim mean by 'fairness, here, is also a kind of blindness'?
options: A single printed price treats four very different customers identically, missing their differences
Fairness makes customers blind to value
questions.explanation: 'A printed number says the same thing to all four. It is fair, yes. But fairness, here, is also a kind of blindness.'

questions.prompt: How does Younes summarise the standoff?
options: Each is half right: the tag is fair but cannot read a face; the haggle reads the face but loses the fearful customers
questions.explanation: 'we are each half right... The tag... cannot read a face. The haggle reads the face and sells a story — and it loses the very people most afraid.'

options: Tag the small cheap items nobody wants a story about; keep haggling for the real pots worth fifteen minutes
Close the stall and reopen elsewhere
questions.explanation: 'The tag for the things that are only things. The haggle for the things that are also a meeting.'

questions.prompt: What does Younes admit he must still learn?
options: To read a face, since he has only ever learned to read a spreadsheet
questions.explanation: 'you will have to teach me to read a face, Uncle, because I have only ever learned to read a spreadsheet.'

questions.prompt: In context, 'the haggle is the thing' (Brahim) means what?
options: The bargaining itself, not the object, is the real product being sold
Haggling is a difficult thing to do
The pot is the only thing that matters
Bargaining is a waste of time
questions.explanation: Brahim insists the tourist buys the experience: 'The pot is the proof. The haggle is the thing.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: Fixed prices and bargaining each gain something and lose something; the wise answer is to match the method to what is really being sold — an object, or a human encounter.
Bargaining should always be replaced by fixed prices.
Tourists should never be allowed to haggle.
Older traders are simply afraid of change.
questions.explanation: The settled compromise: tag the things that are only things, haggle over the things that are also a meeting.

cloze.template:
Younes: Uncle Brahim, I have done the numbers. If we put a fixed {{b1}} on every pot and lamp, we sell faster, the tourists trust us more, and nobody walks away feeling cheated. The shop two doors down did it, and their sales rose.
Uncle Brahim: Their sales have gone up this season. Sit. Before you change a way of selling older than your father, tell me: when a tourist {{b2}} for that copper pot, what has he bought?
Younes: A copper pot.
Uncle Brahim: No. He could buy one at home, by machine, for the same money. What he has bought is fifteen minutes with me — the {{b3}} he will tell: how the old man started high, how he walked away twice, how we shook hands. The pot is the {{b4}}. The haggle is the thing.
Younes: I am not saying the story is worthless. But you are picturing the tourist who enjoys the game. Half of them hate it. They do not know the rules, they are afraid of being {{b5}}, they cannot tell if your 'final price' is fair, so they overpay and feel foolish, or panic and leave with nothing. We lose those sales.
Uncle Brahim: That is true. You have named the real {{b6}}, and I will not pretend it away. The frightened customer is one we lose, and there are more of them now. A tag would catch them.
Younes: So we agree.
Uncle Brahim: We agree on the problem, not yet on the {{b7}}. Think what the tag also removes. When I haggle, I am reading the person. The newlyweds on honeymoon, I let win, and they tell forty friends. The bored rich man, I make work for it, because the work is what he came for. The student counting coins, I find a smaller pot and let him keep his {{b8}}. A printed number says the same to all four — fair, but fairness, here, is a kind of {{b9}}.
Younes: Then we are each half right, and that is uncomfortable. The tag is fair and welcoming and loses no frightened customers — but it cannot read a face. The haggle reads the face and sells a story — but loses the people most afraid of being cheated.
Uncle Brahim: Yes. So perhaps we do not choose. The small things — cheap lamps, keyrings, the tea glasses tourists buy by the dozen — get a tag, because nobody wants a story about a keyring. And the real pots keep the old way. A tag for the things that are only things; the haggle for the things that are also a {{b10}}.
Younes: That I can do the numbers for. But you must teach me to read a face, Uncle, because I have only ever learned to read a {{b11}}.
Uncle Brahim: That, my boy, takes longer than a season, and cannot be done with a calculator. Pour the tea. We will start with the next tourist who touches the copper, and I will tell you, before he opens his mouth, whether he wants the pot or the story.

blanks.explanation: Put a fixed price on every pot.
When a tourist haggles for the pot.
The story he will tell at home.
You have named the real cost.
Agree on the problem, not yet on the cure.
Fairness is also a kind of blindness.
The things that are also a meeting.
I have only ever learned to read a spreadsheet.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Uncle Brahim says some things are 'only things' while others are 'also a meeting'. Think of a purchase in your own life where the human exchange mattered more than the object. Was a fixed price the right way to sell it — or did something get lost?
